- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Similarity audit — macros / macro-clean
2	
3	## Round 1 audit
4	
5	Reviewer: similarity auditor (both-sides). Compared
6	`workspace/macro-clean/src/{lib.rs,ast.rs}` against HASKELL
7	`lib/term/src/Term/Macro.hs`, the macro handling threaded through
8	`lib/theory/src/TheoryObject.hs` and the per-item `applyMacroIn*` functions
9	(`Theory/Model/{Rule,Fact,Formula,Restriction}.hs`, `Lemma.hs`,
10	`ClosedTheory.hs`), and `lib/theory/src/Theory/Text/Parser/Macro.hs`.
11	
12	**Findings: none (0).**
13	
14	The core algorithm is materially different, not a mirror:
15	
16	- Architecture. Haskell has NO single whole-theory expansion pass; macro
17	  application is scattered and lazy — `applyMacroInRule`, `applyMacroInFormula`
18	  (via generic `mapAtoms`), `applyMacroInLemma`, `applyMacroInFact`, etc., each
19	  invoked at different pipeline stages (rule closing, proving, wellformedness,
20	  export) over a flat unexpanded `[Macro]` list. The clean unit implements a
21	  single eager `expand(theory) -> theory` (lib.rs:44) that walks the clean AST
22	  once and drops the declarations — the design the SPEC mandates. The traversal
23	  (`expand_item`/`expand_rule`/`expand_formula`/…) is dictated by the vendored
24	  clean AST (ast.rs), so visiting every term-bearing field is forced, not copied.
25	- Term expansion. Haskell `applyMacros` (Macro.hs:40) keeps bodies unexpanded,
26	  matches a funsym by name+arity (`macroToFunSym`), and RE-RUNS `applyMacros` on
27	  the substituted body to expand nested calls. The clean unit instead
28	  pre-expands every body into a DAG-ordered `MacroTable` (`build_table`,
29	  lib.rs:65) keyed by name, then does a single `substitute_term` with NO re-run
30	  (lib.rs:82,141). Different accounting (memoized table + separate substitution
31	  vs. flat-list lazy re-expansion).
32	
33	Shared shape that is filtered out: "expand args, then substitute formals→args
34	into the body" is the only correct eager, capture-avoiding, parallel-substitution
35	order, dictated by the observed macro semantics ([Q7]); the load-bearing
36	structural choice (pre-expand vs. re-run) differs.
37	
38	Verdict: pass.
39

1	# Unit E: macro expansion
2	
3	Build crate macro-clean in workspace/ implementing .spthy macro semantics as
4	observed: theories in oracle/examples/ use `macros:`; the oracle's
5	pretty-printed output shows their EXPANSION into rules. Characterize: macro
6	definition syntax (incl. arity, nesting, let-style substitution), call-site
7	expansion semantics, name capture/collision behavior (probe edge cases:
8	recursive use, shadowing, arity mismatch errors), and where expansion is
9	visible vs preserved in output. Input model: the theory AST in
10	../wellformedness/interface/ast_types.rs (includes macro fields — study it).
11	Deliverable: expand(theory) -> theory (expanded) with byte-parity fixtures
12	(oracle output on macro theory == oracle output on your pre-expanded
13	equivalent where observable, plus direct expansion checks). BEHAVIOR.md +
14	QUERIES.log + tests; REPORT.md + 10-line summary.
15

1	//! Unit E — `.spthy` macro expansion (clean-room).
2	//!
3	//! `expand(theory)` returns a theory in which every macro use at every use site
4	//! is replaced by its transitively-substituted body, while the `macros:`
5	//! declarations are retained in place (with their original, unexpanded bodies).
6	//! See ../BEHAVIOR.md for the observed semantics this implements; every rule
7	//! below traces to a `[Qn]` observation.
8	//!
9	//! Summary of the semantics implemented here:
10	//!  * a macro `name(f1..fk) = body`; call `name(a1..ak)` binds `fi := ai` and
11	//!    substitutes **simultaneously** (parallel / capture-avoiding)          [Q7]
12	//!  * a bare, untagged name equal to a NULLARY macro is a parenthesis-free use
13	//!    of that macro and resolves to its body; `~x`/`$x` and names of arity>=1
14	//!    macros are ordinary variables and do not resolve                      [Q32,Q33,Q34,Q35]
15	//!  * a formal matches a body variable by **full identity incl. sort**; `~x`
16	//!    and `$x` do NOT match an untagged formal `x` and stay free            [Q27,Q28]
17	//!  * a nullary macro reserves its name against a same-named formal: inside a
18	//!    body the name resolves to the macro, not the formal                   [Q36]
19	//!  * bodies may call only strictly-earlier macros ⇒ the macro dependency
20	//!    graph is a DAG, so expansion always terminates                        [Q8,Q19,Q20]
21	//!  * expansion is transitive: a body's own macro calls are expanded too    [Q9,Q18]
22	//!  * at the AST level a call's arg-count already equals the macro arity
23	//!    (the parser packs/rejects mismatches); `expand` no-ops on a mismatch)  [Q11,Q12,Q15,Q16,Q17]
24	//!  * the `macros:` declaration block is preserved unchanged in the output;
25	//!    the reference retains it in its pretty output and the consuming
26	//!    pipeline requires it in place                                          [Q37]
27	
28	pub mod ast;
29	
30	use std::collections::HashMap;
31	use ast::*;
32	
33	/// Table of macros whose bodies are already fully expanded (macro-free),
34	/// keyed by macro name. Formals are matched against body variables by full
35	/// `VarSpec` identity, so the substitution map is keyed by `VarSpec`.
36	#[derive(Default)]
37	struct MacroTable {
38	    by_name: HashMap<String, (Vec<VarSpec>, Term)>,
39	}
40	
41	impl MacroTable {
42	    fn get(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&(Vec<VarSpec>, Term)> {
43	        self.by_name.get(name)
44	    }
45	}
46	
47	/// Expand every macro use in `theory`, keeping the `macros:` declarations in
48	/// place.
49	///
50	/// The result, fed back to the oracle, reproduces the reasoning content the
51	/// oracle computes for the original (modulo-AC variants / expanded-formula /
52	/// guarded-formula), which is byte-identical to a hand-inlined equivalent
53	/// (verified by workspace/byteparity.sh and workspace/formula_parity.sh). The
54	/// `macros:` declaration items pass through unchanged (with their original,
55	/// unexpanded bodies): the reference retains the block in its pretty output and
56	/// the consuming pipeline requires it in place [Q37].
57	pub fn expand(theory: &Theory) -> Theory {
58	    let table = build_table(theory);
59	    let items = theory.items.iter().map(|it| expand_item(it, &table)).collect();
60	    Theory {
61	        is_diff: theory.is_diff,
62	        name: theory.name.clone(),
63	        configuration: theory.configuration.clone(),
64	        items,
65	    }
66	}
67	
68	/// Build the macro table with fully-expanded (macro-free) bodies.
69	///
70	/// Macros are processed in declaration order; each body is expanded against the
71	/// macros defined strictly before it. Forward/self/mutual references are parse
72	/// errors upstream [Q8,Q19,Q20], so "earlier only" is complete and cannot loop.
73	fn build_table(theory: &Theory) -> MacroTable {
74	    let mut table = MacroTable::default();
75	    for item in &theory.items {
76	        if let TheoryItem::Macros(ms) = item {
77	            for m in ms {
78	                let body = expand_term(&m.body, &table);
79	                table.by_name.insert(m.name.clone(), (m.args.clone(), body));
80	            }
81	        }
82	    }
83	    table
84	}
85	
86	// ---- term-level expansion -------------------------------------------------
87	
88	/// Bottom-up macro expansion of a term. Arguments are expanded first, then a
89	/// macro call is replaced by its body with formals bound to the expanded args.
90	fn expand_term(t: &Term, table: &MacroTable) -> Term {
91	    match t {
92	        // A bare, untagged name equal to a NULLARY macro is that macro used
93	        // without parentheses; resolve it to the (already-expanded) body. A
94	        // fresh/pub-sorted name or the name of an arity>=1 macro is an ordinary
95	        // variable and is left untouched [Q32,Q33,Q34,Q35]. A nullary macro
96	        // reserves its name even against a same-named formal [Q36].
97	        Term::Var(v) => match table.get(&v.name) {
98	            Some((formals, body)) if v.sort == SortHint::Untagged && formals.is_empty() => {
99	                body.clone()
100	            }
101	            _ => t.clone(),
102	        },
103	
104	        Term::PubLit(_)
105	        | Term::FreshLit(_)
106	        | Term::NatLit(_)
107	        | Term::Number(_)
108	        | Term::NumberOne
109	        | Term::NatOne
110	        | Term::DhNeutral => t.clone(),
111	
112	        Term::App(name, args) => {
113	            let args: Vec<Term> = args.iter().map(|a| expand_term(a, table)).collect();
114	            if let Some((formals, body)) = table.get(name) {
115	                // At the AST level arg-count == arity for a valid parse. If it
116	                // ever differs (defensive), leave the call unexpanded.
117	                if formals.len() == args.len() {
118	                    let subst = build_subst(formals, &args);
119	                    // `body` is already macro-free; a simultaneous substitution
120	                    // of macro-free args therefore yields a macro-free term.
121	                    return substitute_term(body, &subst);
122	                }
123	            }
124	            Term::App(name.clone(), args)
125	        }
126	
127	        Term::AlgApp(name, a, b) => Term::AlgApp(
128	            name.clone(),
129	            Box::new(expand_term(a, table)),
130	            Box::new(expand_term(b, table)),
131	        ),
132	        Term::Pair(ts) => Term::Pair(ts.iter().map(|x| expand_term(x, table)).collect()),
133	        Term::Diff(a, b) => Term::Diff(
134	            Box::new(expand_term(a, table)),
135	            Box::new(expand_term(b, table)),
136	        ),
137	        Term::BinOp(op, a, b) => Term::BinOp(
138	            *op,
139	            Box::new(expand_term(a, table)),
140	            Box::new(expand_term(b, table)),
141	        ),
142	        Term::PatMatch(a) => Term::PatMatch(Box::new(expand_term(a, table))),
143	    }
144	}
145	
146	/// Build the simultaneous substitution `formal_i := arg_i`, keyed by the full
147	/// `VarSpec` of each formal (so sort/idx participate in matching) [Q27,Q28].
148	fn build_subst(formals: &[VarSpec], args: &[Term]) -> HashMap<VarSpec, Term> {
149	    formals
150	        .iter()
151	        .cloned()
152	        .zip(args.iter().cloned())
153	        .collect()
154	}
155	
156	/// Apply a simultaneous substitution to `t`. A `Var` that matches a key is
157	/// replaced by the bound term **without** re-descending into it (parallel
158	/// substitution — no capture) [Q7]. Non-matching variables (incl. differently
159	/// sorted `~x`/`$x`) are left untouched [Q27,Q28].
160	fn substitute_term(t: &Term, subst: &HashMap<VarSpec, Term>) -> Term {
161	    match t {
162	        Term::Var(v) => match subst.get(v) {
163	            Some(bound) => bound.clone(),
164	            None => t.clone(),
165	        },
166	        Term::PubLit(_)
167	        | Term::FreshLit(_)
168	        | Term::NatLit(_)
169	        | Term::Number(_)
170	        | Term::NumberOne
171	        | Term::NatOne
172	        | Term::DhNeutral => t.clone(),
173	        Term::App(name, args) => Term::App(
174	            name.clone(),
175	            args.iter().map(|a| substitute_term(a, subst)).collect(),
176	        ),
177	        Term::AlgApp(name, a, b) => Term::AlgApp(
178	            name.clone(),
179	            Box::new(substitute_term(a, subst)),
180	            Box::new(substitute_term(b, subst)),
181	        ),
182	        Term::Pair(ts) => Term::Pair(ts.iter().map(|x| substitute_term(x, subst)).collect()),
183	        Term::Diff(a, b) => Term::Diff(
184	            Box::new(substitute_term(a, subst)),
185	            Box::new(substitute_term(b, subst)),
186	        ),
187	        Term::BinOp(op, a, b) => Term::BinOp(
188	            *op,
189	            Box::new(substitute_term(a, subst)),
190	            Box::new(substitute_term(b, subst)),
191	        ),
192	        Term::PatMatch(a) => Term::PatMatch(Box::new(substitute_term(a, subst))),
193	    }
194	}
195	
196	// ---- structural recursion through the theory ------------------------------
197	
198	fn expand_fact(f: &Fact, table: &MacroTable) -> Fact {
199	    Fact {
200	        persistent: f.persistent,
201	        name: f.name.clone(),
202	        args: f.args.iter().map(|a| expand_term(a, table)).collect(),
203	        annotations: f.annotations.clone(),
204	    }
205	}
206	
207	fn expand_atom(a: &Atom, table: &MacroTable) -> Atom {
208	    match a {
209	        Atom::Eq(x, y) => Atom::Eq(expand_term(x, table), expand_term(y, table)),
210	        Atom::Less(x, y) => Atom::Less(expand_term(x, table), expand_term(y, table)),
211	        Atom::LessMset(x, y) => Atom::LessMset(expand_term(x, table), expand_term(y, table)),
212	        Atom::Subterm(x, y) => Atom::Subterm(expand_term(x, table), expand_term(y, table)),
213	        Atom::Action(f, t) => Atom::Action(expand_fact(f, table), expand_term(t, table)),
214	        Atom::Last(t) => Atom::Last(expand_term(t, table)),
215	        Atom::Pred(f) => Atom::Pred(expand_fact(f, table)),
216	    }
217	}
218	
219	fn expand_formula(phi: &Formula, table: &MacroTable) -> Formula {
220	    match phi {
221	        Formula::False => Formula::False,
222	        Formula::True => Formula::True,
223	        Formula::Atom(a) => Formula::Atom(expand_atom(a, table)),
224	        Formula::Not(p) => Formula::Not(Box::new(expand_formula(p, table))),
225	        Formula::And(p, q) => Formula::And(
226	            Box::new(expand_formula(p, table)),
227	            Box::new(expand_formula(q, table)),
228	        ),
229	        Formula::Or(p, q) => Formula::Or(
230	            Box::new(expand_formula(p, table)),
231	            Box::new(expand_formula(q, table)),
232	        ),
233	        Formula::Implies(p, q) => Formula::Implies(
234	            Box::new(expand_formula(p, table)),
235	            Box::new(expand_formula(q, table)),
236	        ),
237	        Formula::Iff(p, q) => Formula::Iff(
238	            Box::new(expand_formula(p, table)),
239	            Box::new(expand_formula(q, table)),
240	        ),
241	        Formula::Forall(vs, p) => {
242	            Formula::Forall(vs.clone(), Box::new(expand_formula(p, table)))
243	        }
244	        Formula::Exists(vs, p) => {
245	            Formula::Exists(vs.clone(), Box::new(expand_formula(p, table)))
246	        }
247	    }
248	}
249	
250	fn expand_let(b: &LetBinding, table: &MacroTable) -> LetBinding {
251	    // A macro call may appear in the let value [Q23]; the bound pattern `var`
252	    // is a variable/pattern and is expanded too for uniformity.
253	    LetBinding {
254	        var: expand_term(&b.var, table),
255	        value: expand_term(&b.value, table),
256	    }
257	}
258	
259	fn expand_rule(r: &Rule, table: &MacroTable) -> Rule {
260	    Rule {
261	        name: r.name.clone(),
262	        modulo: r.modulo.clone(),
263	        attributes: r.attributes.clone(),
264	        let_block: r.let_block.iter().map(|b| expand_let(b, table)).collect(),
265	        premises: r.premises.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
266	        actions: r.actions.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
267	        conclusions: r.conclusions.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
268	        embedded_restrictions: r
269	            .embedded_restrictions
270	            .iter()
271	            .map(|p| expand_formula(p, table))
272	            .collect(),
273	        variants: r.variants.iter().map(|v| expand_rule(v, table)).collect(),
274	        left_right: r.left_right.as_ref().map(|(l, rr)| {
275	            (
276	                Box::new(expand_rule(l, table)),
277	                Box::new(expand_rule(rr, table)),
278	            )
279	        }),
280	    }
281	}
282	
283	fn expand_sapic_action(a: &SapicAction, table: &MacroTable) -> SapicAction {
284	    match a {
285	        SapicAction::New(v) => SapicAction::New(v.clone()),
286	        SapicAction::Insert(k, v) => {
287	            SapicAction::Insert(expand_term(k, table), expand_term(v, table))
288	        }
289	        SapicAction::Delete(k) => SapicAction::Delete(expand_term(k, table)),
290	        SapicAction::ChIn { chan, msg } => SapicAction::ChIn {
291	            chan: chan.as_ref().map(|c| expand_term(c, table)),
292	            msg: expand_term(msg, table),
293	        },
294	        SapicAction::ChOut { chan, msg } => SapicAction::ChOut {
295	            chan: chan.as_ref().map(|c| expand_term(c, table)),
296	            msg: expand_term(msg, table),
297	        },
298	        SapicAction::Lock(t) => SapicAction::Lock(expand_term(t, table)),
299	        SapicAction::Unlock(t) => SapicAction::Unlock(expand_term(t, table)),
300	        SapicAction::Event(f) => SapicAction::Event(expand_fact(f, table)),
301	        SapicAction::Msr { prems, acts, concs, restrictions } => SapicAction::Msr {
302	            prems: prems.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
303	            acts: acts.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
304	            concs: concs.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
305	            restrictions: restrictions.iter().map(|p| expand_formula(p, table)).collect(),
306	        },
307	    }
308	}
309	
310	fn expand_condition(c: &Condition, table: &MacroTable) -> Condition {
311	    match c {
312	        Condition::Eq(x, y) => Condition::Eq(expand_term(x, table), expand_term(y, table)),
313	        Condition::Formula(p) => Condition::Formula(expand_formula(p, table)),
314	    }
315	}
316	
317	fn expand_comb(c: &ProcessComb, table: &MacroTable) -> ProcessComb {
318	    match c {
319	        ProcessComb::Parallel => ProcessComb::Parallel,
320	        ProcessComb::Ndc => ProcessComb::Ndc,
321	        ProcessComb::Cond(cond) => ProcessComb::Cond(expand_condition(cond, table)),
322	        ProcessComb::Lookup(t, v) => ProcessComb::Lookup(expand_term(t, table), v.clone()),
323	        ProcessComb::Let { pat, value } => ProcessComb::Let {
324	            pat: expand_term(pat, table),
325	            value: expand_term(value, table),
326	        },
327	    }
328	}
329	
330	fn expand_process(p: &Process, table: &MacroTable) -> Process {
331	    match p {
332	        Process::Null => Process::Null,
333	        Process::Action { action, body } => Process::Action {
334	            action: expand_sapic_action(action, table),
335	            body: Box::new(expand_process(body, table)),
336	        },
337	        Process::Comb { comb, left, right } => Process::Comb {
338	            comb: expand_comb(comb, table),
339	            left: Box::new(expand_process(left, table)),
340	            right: Box::new(expand_process(right, table)),
341	        },
342	        Process::Replication(b) => Process::Replication(Box::new(expand_process(b, table))),
343	        Process::Call { name, args } => Process::Call {
344	            name: name.clone(),
345	            args: args.iter().map(|a| expand_term(a, table)).collect(),
346	        },
347	        Process::AtAnnotation(b, t) => {
348	            Process::AtAnnotation(Box::new(expand_process(b, table)), expand_term(t, table))
349	        }
350	    }
351	}
352	
353	fn expand_restriction(r: &Restriction, table: &MacroTable) -> Restriction {
354	    Restriction {
355	        name: r.name.clone(),
356	        formula: expand_formula(&r.formula, table),
357	        attributes: r.attributes.clone(),
358	    }
359	}
360	
361	fn expand_lemma(l: &Lemma, table: &MacroTable) -> Lemma {
362	    Lemma {
363	        name: l.name.clone(),
364	        modulo: l.modulo.clone(),
365	        attributes: l.attributes.clone(),
366	        trace_quantifier: l.trace_quantifier.clone(),
367	        formula: expand_formula(&l.formula, table),
368	        proof: l.proof.clone(),
369	        plaintext: l.plaintext.clone(),
370	    }
371	}
372	
373	fn expand_predicate(p: &Predicate, table: &MacroTable) -> Predicate {
374	    Predicate {
375	        fact: expand_fact(&p.fact, table),
376	        formula: expand_formula(&p.formula, table),
377	    }
378	}
379	
380	fn expand_item(it: &TheoryItem, table: &MacroTable) -> TheoryItem {
381	    match it {
382	        TheoryItem::Rule(r) => TheoryItem::Rule(expand_rule(r, table)),
383	        TheoryItem::IntrRule(r) => TheoryItem::IntrRule(expand_rule(r, table)),
384	        TheoryItem::Restriction(r) => TheoryItem::Restriction(expand_restriction(r, table)),
385	        TheoryItem::LegacyAxiom(r) => TheoryItem::LegacyAxiom(expand_restriction(r, table)),
386	        TheoryItem::Lemma(l) => TheoryItem::Lemma(expand_lemma(l, table)),
387	        TheoryItem::AccLemma(a) => TheoryItem::AccLemma(AccLemma {
388	            name: a.name.clone(),
389	            attributes: a.attributes.clone(),
390	            formula: expand_formula(&a.formula, table),
391	            case_test_idents: a.case_test_idents.clone(),
392	        }),
393	        TheoryItem::CaseTest(c) => TheoryItem::CaseTest(CaseTest {
394	            name: c.name.clone(),
395	            formula: expand_formula(&c.formula, table),
396	        }),
397	        TheoryItem::Predicates(ps) => {
398	            TheoryItem::Predicates(ps.iter().map(|p| expand_predicate(p, table)).collect())
399	        }
400	        TheoryItem::ProcessDef(pd) => TheoryItem::ProcessDef(ProcessDef {
401	            name: pd.name.clone(),
402	            vars: pd.vars.clone(),
403	            body: expand_process(&pd.body, table),
404	        }),
405	        TheoryItem::TopLevelProcess(p) => {
406	            TheoryItem::TopLevelProcess(expand_process(p, table))
407	        }
408	        TheoryItem::EquivLemma(a, b) => {
409	            TheoryItem::EquivLemma(expand_process(a, table), expand_process(b, table))
410	        }
411	        TheoryItem::DiffEquivLemma(p) => {
412	            TheoryItem::DiffEquivLemma(expand_process(p, table))
413	        }
414	        // Equations carry terms; expand uniformly (a no-op when they contain no
415	        // macro call — not observed in the corpus, but handled for robustness).
416	        TheoryItem::Equations { convergent, eqs } => TheoryItem::Equations {
417	            convergent: *convergent,
418	            eqs: eqs
419	                .iter()
420	                .map(|e| Equation {
421	                    lhs: expand_term(&e.lhs, table),
422	                    rhs: expand_term(&e.rhs, table),
423	                })
424	                .collect(),
425	        },
426	        // Items with no macro-bearing terms are carried through unchanged. Macros
427	        // items are filtered out before this function is called.
428	        TheoryItem::Macros(_)
429	        | TheoryItem::Builtins(_)
430	        | TheoryItem::Functions(_)
431	        | TheoryItem::Options(_)
432	        | TheoryItem::Heuristic(_)
433	        | TheoryItem::Tactic(_)
434	        | TheoryItem::DiffLemma(_)
435	        | TheoryItem::Export { .. }
436	        | TheoryItem::FormalComment { .. }
437	        | TheoryItem::Define(_)
438	        | TheoryItem::Include(_) => it.clone(),
439	    }
440	}

+      Purpose: does a bare name equal to an arity>=1 macro resolve?
+      Observed: NO. `A( m )` is left as-is; `m` is an unbound variable (trivial
+      AC variant, unbound-variable warning). => only a NULLARY macro is resolved
+      from a bare name.
+
+[Q36] probes/formal_vs_nullary.spthy: macros: base()=h('k'), f(base)=<base,base>;
+      call f(a).  Purpose: when a formal name equals an existing nullary macro,
+      which wins inside the body? Observed: modulo-AC A( <h('k'), h('k')> ) — the
+      NULLARY MACRO wins; the formal `base` and the argument `a` are dropped.
+      => a nullary macro reserves its name against a same-named formal; a body's
+      bare occurrences resolve to the macro before formal binding.

+      captures/example_issue777.out, probes/acc_macro full close).
+      Purpose: does the reference retain the macros: block after processing?
+      Observed: YES — the pretty output always reprints `macros: <name>( args )
+      = <body>, ...` with the ORIGINAL (un-expanded) bodies, regardless of how
+      the uses are expanded elsewhere. => the declaration block is preserved in
+      place; `expand` keeps the Macros items unchanged.
+

+      vs acc_both_expanded (full accountability close via formula_parity.sh).
+      macro `af(x)=h(x)` used in a `... accounts for "..."` formula.
+      Purpose: does the macro stage expand acc-lemma formulas, and at what
+      stage? Observed: --parse-only preserves the call (Unequal( af(sid) )). On
+      close the accountability lemma is translated into generated lemmas whose
+      PRIMARY formulas still show the call (Unequal( af(sid) )) and whose
+      `/* guarded formula characterizing ... */` shows the EXPANDED form
+      (Unequal( h(sid) )). formula_parity.sh: the macro theory's guarded content
+      is byte-identical to the hand-inlined equivalent's. => macros ARE expanded
+      in acc-lemma formulas, visible at the guarded/processing stage (same
+      pattern as ordinary lemmas [Q4]).
+
+[Q39] fixtures/casetest_macro.spthy vs casetest_expanded.spthy; acc_both pair.
+      macro `ct(x)=h(x)` used in a `test <name>: "..."` case-test formula.
+      Purpose: same probe for case tests. Observed: --parse-only preserves
+      Blame( ct(sid), x ). On close the case test feeds the generated
+      `predicate: Blamed(x) <=> ... Blame( ct(sid), x ) ...` (call kept) and the
+      generated lemmas' guarded formulas show Blame( h(sid), x ) (expanded).
+      formula_parity.sh: guarded content byte-identical to the hand-inlined
+      equivalent. => macros ARE expanded in case-test formulas at the
+      guarded/processing stage.

+      rule fixtures (incl. bare_nullary) + 4 formula_parity fixtures (lemmas,
+      casetest, acclemma, acc_both). Result: ALL PASS.

+# theories (its reduced env lacks a UTF-8 locale, so the reference aborts on the
+# ∀/∃ logic glyphs before generating lemmas). formula_parity.sh runs the oracle
+# with a UTF-8 locale and compares only the guarded/expanded-formula blocks, so

+## Round 4 — three expansion-semantics gaps + declaration preservation
+
+Closed three gaps and reconciled the declaration-preservation interop contract.
+Every claim traces to a round-4 oracle probe ([Q32]–[Q40], `probes/`, `captures/`).
+
+**GAP 1 — bare nullary macro uses.** A 0-ary macro used as a plain name (no
+parentheses) is a use of that macro, treated identically to the parenthesised
+form: `konst()=h('k')`, bare `konst` → `h('k')` [Q32]. Characterised the exact
+resolution conditions: it fires in every term position and transitively through
+bodies [Q32,Q33]; **untagged sort only** (`~konst`/`$konst` stay ordinary vars,
+and a macro-name use can't be sort-annotated) [Q34,Q32]; **nullary only** (a bare
+name for an arity ≥ 1 macro stays an ordinary variable) [Q35]; and a nullary
+macro **reserves its name against a same-named formal** [Q36]. Per the interface
+contract such a use reaches `expand` as `Term::Var("konst")`; `expand_term` now
+resolves an untagged `Var` whose name is a nullary macro to that macro's body —
+and the existing "expand body first, then bind formals" order reproduces the
+name-reservation result [Q36] by construction.
+
+**GAP 2 — accountability-lemma formulas** and **GAP 3 — case-test formulas.**
+The reference's macro stage DOES expand macros used in `... accounts for "..."`
+acc-lemma formulas and `test <name>: "..."` case-test formulas. Stage behaviour:
+`--parse-only` preserves the call; on close the acc lemma is translated into
+generated lemmas (+`predicate:`) whose primary renderings keep the call and whose
+`/* guarded formula ... */` blocks show the expansion — the exact
+primary-keeps-call / guarded-shows-expansion pattern of ordinary lemmas [Q4].
+Confirmed with identity and non-identity (`h(x)`) macros [Q38,Q39]. `expand`
+already rewrites `AccLemma.formula` and `CaseTest.formula`; added parity fixtures
+prove it.
+
+**Declaration preservation.** Probed that the reference retains the `macros:`
+block (with original, un-expanded bodies) in its pretty output after processing
+[Q37]. Per the interop contract, `expand` now **preserves the `Macros` items in
+place** (previously it dropped them); only use sites are expanded.
+
+**New harness — `formula_parity.sh`.** `byteparity.sh`'s env-stripping approach
+cannot exercise accountability theories (its reduced env lacks a UTF-8 locale, so
+the reference aborts on ∀/∃ glyphs before generating lemmas). `formula_parity.sh`
+runs the oracle with a UTF-8 locale and compares only the guarded/expanded-formula
+blocks — the harness of record for the lemma/acc-lemma/case-test gaps.
+
+**Acceptance (round 4).** `run_tests.sh`: **21** cargo direct-expansion tests
+(14 prior — one renamed `drops_→preserves_macro_declarations` and one fixture
+updated for preservation — plus 7 new: 5 bare-nullary + acc-lemma + case-test),
+7 byteparity rule fixtures (incl. `bare_nullary`), and 4 formula_parity fixtures
+(`lemmas`, `casetest`, `acclemma`, `acc_both`). **ALL PASS** [Q40].

+Fixtures added: `fixtures/{bare_nullary,casetest,acclemma,acc_both}_{macro,expanded}.spthy`;
+captures in `captures/{bare_nullary_*,acc_both_*,bare_sorts,bare_nonnullary,formal_vs_nullary}.out`.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Formula-context parity harness for Unit E (macro expansion).
#
# For contexts where macro expansion surfaces as a *processed formula* rather
# than an AC rule variant -- lemmas, restrictions, accountability lemmas, and
# case tests -- the oracle prints the un-expanded macro CALL in the primary
# rendering and the fully EXPANDED term in a `guarded formula ...` (or
# `expanded formula:`) comment block. Macro expansion is responsible for that
# expanded content.
#
# This harness runs the black-box oracle (full env, so Unicode logic output
# encodes correctly) on a (macro-theory, hand-inlined-equivalent) pair and
# compares ONLY the expanded/guarded-formula content. Both theories compute the
# same expanded content, so the blocks must be byte-identical. Exit 0 == parity.
#
# Usage: formula_parity.sh <macro.spthy> <expanded.spthy>
set -u
HERE="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
ORACLE="$HERE/../oracle/hs_oracle.sh"

python3 - "$1" "$2" "$ORACLE" <<'PY'
import subprocess, sys, re
macro_f, exp_f, oracle = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3]

def run(f):
    return subprocess.run(
        ["bash", oracle, f], capture_output=True, text=True,
        env={"ORACLE_TIMEOUT": "120", "PATH": "/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:/usr/bin:/bin",
             "LC_ALL": "C.UTF-8", "LANG": "C.UTF-8"},
    ).stdout.splitlines()

HDR = re.compile(r'guarded formula characterizing|^\s*expanded formula:')

def blocks(lines):
    """Concatenate every guarded/expanded-formula block: the lines from just
    after such a header up to the closing '*/'. Whitespace-normalised."""
    out, i, n = [], 0, len(lines)
    while i < n:
        if HDR.search(lines[i]):
            i += 1
            while i < n and lines[i].strip() != '*/':
                s = lines[i].strip()
                if s:
                    out.append(re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', s))
                i += 1
        else:
            i += 1
    return out

bm = blocks(run(macro_f))
be = blocks(run(exp_f))
if not bm:
    print("NO EXPANDED/GUARDED BLOCKS FOUND (oracle produced none)")
    sys.exit(2)
if bm == be:
    print("FORMULA-PARITY OK: expanded content identical (%d block-lines)" % len(bm))
    sys.exit(0)
print("FORMULA-PARITY MISMATCH")
import difflib
for d in difflib.unified_diff(bm, be, "macro-guarded", "expanded-guarded", lineterm=""):
    print(d)
sys.exit(1)
PY

Reviewer: similarity auditor (both-sides). Scope: THIS round's delta only, from
clean-room HEAD `63ed8a9`. Restricted `git diff`/untracked set under `macros/`:
`workspace/macro-clean/src/{lib.rs,tests.rs}`, `BEHAVIOR.md`, `QUERIES.log`,

the parser's nullary/signature machinery
(`Theory/Text/Parser/{Term.hs (`nullaryApp`, l.143), Token.hs
(`addMacrosToSignature`, `macroToFunSym`, l.197)}`); the item-level appliers
(`applyMacroIn{Rule,Fact,Formula,Restriction,Lemma}` and their call sites in
`ClosedTheory.hs`, `Prover.hs`, `Parser.hs`, `Wellformedness.hs`,
`Model/Formula.hs`).

Delta content: (a) bare untagged nullary-macro name resolution [Q32–Q36];
(b) `Macros` items now preserved in place instead of dropped [Q37]; (c) new
probes/fixtures for acc-lemma [Q38] & case-test [Q39] formula expansion (the
`expand_item` arms for `AccLemma`/`CaseTest` were already present in round 1 —
this round only adds evidence, no new code path); (d) `formula_parity.sh`.

**Findings: none (0).** No copied protectable expression in the delta.

Abstraction-filtration-comparison of the delta:

- Bare-nullary resolution is a genuinely DIFFERENT mechanism, not a mirror. The
  reference resolves a parenthesis-free nullary use at PARSE time: macro names
  are injected into the Maude signature as arity-0 `NoEq`/Private/Destructor
  funsyms (`addMacroSym` via `macroToFunSym`), and `nullaryApp` (Term.hs:143)
  matches a bare token against `funSyms ∪ macroNames`, emitting an `FApp fs []`;
  `applyMacros` then expands it as an ordinary call. The clean unit has no
  signature and no parser; it resolves at expansion time a `Term::Var` whose
  `sort == Untagged` and whose name is a **nullary** table entry (lib.rs:97-102).
  Sort-gating (`~x`/`$x` excluded) and nullary-gating are behavior-dictated
  (observed: `bare_sorts.out` keeps `~konst`/`$konst`; `bare_nonnullary.out`
  keeps `A( m )` as an unbound var) — the load-bearing choice (where and how the
  bare name becomes a use) differs from the reference.
- Name-reservation over a same-named formal [Q36] is likewise reached by a
  different route. The reference gets it for free from the parser: inside a body
  a name equal to an earlier nullary macro parses as `FApp base []`, never as a
  bound var, so the formal can't match. The clean unit gets the same observable
  result from its "expand each body against strictly-earlier macros BEFORE
  binding formals" order (`build_table` lib.rs:73-84 + the Var branch): a
  documented round-1 structural choice, not copied. `formal_vs_nullary.out`
  dictates the result (`A( <h('k'), h('k')> )`, formal+arg dropped).
- Preserve-in-place [Q37] is behavior-dictated: every full-close capture
